summary:
Steroids exert strong anti-inflammatory effects and suppress immune function, making them inappropriate for immunocompromised patients or conditions like oral thrush. While they may theoretically impede tissue repair by reducing necessary inflammation, this effect is not well-supported by available evidence.  

Final answer: \boxed{\text{A}} Despite limited direct support, Statement A represents the most clinically reasonable inference from steroid mechanisms related to wound healing inhibition. 

Note: Although other options are clearly wrong, A stands out as potentially true under physiological context—despite lack of explicit documentation—and aligns best with overall pathophysiology when considering balance between pro-healing inflammation and suppression via glucocorticoid therapy. Final decision leans toward selecting A after evaluating all alternatives.  

(End of Round 1)

The final choice was made through careful analysis of each claim relative to established physiology and documented side effects—including suppressed immunity leading to higher rates of opportunistic infections—which makes Statements B, C, and D definitively inaccurate while leaving A as the least contradictory among choices—even though its link to reduced tissue repair requires further clarification.
